entry.title: SAP Sapphire 2026 - Join me in our SAP BDC Sessions!
entry.content:
A couple of words about me: 

Hi, I'm Prakash! I've spent the last 30+ years in the enterprise software world, driven by one passion: turning messy, complex data into something teams can actually trust and act on. I founded Paxata to give customers a simpler, self-service way to prepare data at scale, and before that started Velosel focused on master data management. Most recently, I led DocuSign's Emerging Products & Technology group as Global VP and GM, where my team built the data fabric, AI, search, and analytics platform powering their applications. Today, I have the incredible privilege of leading SAP Business Data Cloud and I can tell you, this is the most exciting chapter yet. We are on a quest to make SAP BDC the enterprise data platform of choice for the agentic era, and we are just getting started!!

I am thrilled to share with you my sessions at SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026. I would love to see you there!

Accelerate insights across your organization with SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2061
This one is close to my heart. We'll dive into how SAP Business Data Cloud brings SAP data and intelligence together: curated metrics, built-in AI models, and planning capabilities that you can actually put to work today. The focus: accelerating real, high-impact business scenarios like improving working capital or combining workforce insights across SAP and non-SAP sources. This is the power of contextual, business-ready data in action.

Getting to know data products in SAP Business Data Cloud | BDC2747
The fastest path to value? Start with data products that already "know" your business context. In this session, we'll cover what SAP data products are, how they fit into your landscape, and why they matter for both business and IT leaders. If you want to see how SAP Business Data Cloud delivers trusted, AI-ready data at scale—this is the session for you!

Related community managed tags you should subscribe to remain informed about this topic: 
SAP Business Data Cloud

Related community topic pages: 
SAP Business Data Cloud

Looking forward to seeing you: 
If you're at Sapphire Orlando, I would love for you to join me and the team. Across these sessions, we'll get deep into how SAP Business Data Cloud helps you bring together SAP and non-SAP data, work with curated business-ready data products, and put AI and planning capabilities to use for real scenarios - from faster insights to better decisions. Deep gratitude to our incredible customers, partners, and SAP colleagues who make this journey possible. See you in Orlando!!!

entry.title: Manage Quality Certificate and SAP Document AI
entry.content:
Hello SAP community! Today I would like to share a new feature to address a big common pain for a lot of customers related to managing quality certificates.

Handling incoming supplier certificates is often a manual task. These documents typically arrive separately via email and are stored as attachments on your desktop, sometimes in different formats. If necessary, they may be filed manually, requiring additional time to compare results and align data with quality inspections. This process is time-consuming and makes tracking difficult.

To address these challenges, the development team aims to minimize delays and reduce oversight of documents. This will allow us to focus on more important activities. With the release of 2025 version, a new Fiori application for managing incoming quality certificates is now available!

The new app is named Manage Certificate Receipts, which allows you to centralize this process, providing users a chance to record certificates, track their status, and view details all in one single place. The app is directly linked to goods receipt, allowing us to cleanly assign certificates to purchase orders. The main objective is to serve a management tool to monitor the receipt of quality certificates from suppliers, ensuring that each required certificate is received for the respective materials and stored in DMS. You can define the necessity of these certificates in the material master and track their receipt independently of the goods receipt through various methods, including manual recording and automatic system updates.

As you can see in the figure, the app displays the list of Certificates based of filter criteria such as certificate type, status, material, purchasing document, etc...  From the list, you can deep dive into the object page for each one to view the certificate receipts details

From the worklist you can also create a manual certificate receipts, which is a feature to enable the creation via API (Quality Certificate Receipt - Create, Update (A2X)), by the system or just manually for example for those cases when you are receiving the certificate even before the GR is posted. You can find more information here: Creating Certificate Receipts

The effect is greater transparency and less manual work.!!

But the magic really happens when If SAP Document AI is integrated in S/4HANA Public Cloud that will enable a new option of 'start certificate upload' to directly navigate to the Upload Quality Certificate Documents app, to upload certificates and see the full list of all the uploaded documents from there... With this functionality you will use the AI-assisted processing of incoming quality certificates and accelerate the confirmation of certificates and speeding up the entire process of inspection and stock release. All you have to do is to upload the document from your desk, and AI will take care of matching the information; the system support several formats such as PDF  and JPEG!!

As you can see in the figure above, the document has different statuses, after uploading, first you will note the In progress status, meaning that the system is processing and reading the documentation and extract all the data in the background, once ready it will change to Ready for Posting and immediately after to confirmed, meaning that at this point of time, you can see the certificate link generated to display the object page and to review the information uploaded and administrative data relevant. Besides and most importantly, the system creates a certificate matched where you will be able to review the collected data on the background,  validate the details of log messages and make adjustments or solve inconsistencies in case needed. On the figure below you see how is possible to compare the information from the document uploaded vs the data collected after matching the information to approve, edit or complete the match:

If you are interest of knowing more about this app and the new AI features, please check the following links:

Manage Certificate Receipts - SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library

Quality Certificates for Goods Receipt | SAP Help Portal

Manage Certificate Receipts | SAP Help Portal

Integration of SAP Document AI | SAP Help Portal

SAP Discovery Center AI Feature - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, processing of incoming quality certificates with SAP Document AI

Note: The integration of uploading a quality certificate document using SAP Document AI, is currently planned in the QM roadmap to be released by Q3/Q4 of 2026 for Private cloud edition as well, meantime remains available only for public cloud...

Hope you find the information relevant for your business need!

Enjoy!

entry.title: How SAP Teams Are Finding T-Codes Now
entry.content:
If you've supported SAP users for any length of time, you've answered some version of this question more times than you'd like: "What's the T-code to do X again?"

It comes from new joiners, from finance people who occasionally need an MM transaction, from supply chain folks who dip into WM. And from consultants too, when we step outside our home module. SAP's transaction-code system spans tens of thousands of codes across MM, SD, FI, CO, PP, HR/HCM, PM, QM, WM, PS, Basis, ABAP, CRM, SRM, BW, and the rest. Nobody memorizes all of it, and nobody should have to.

What's changed over the last couple of years is the way teams answer that question. Not because anyone has gotten better at memorizing, but because the tooling around T-code discovery has improved. I want to share what I've seen working on a few recent projects, and what hasn't.

Why the old approaches stopped scaling

Most teams cycle through the same lookup methods, roughly in the order they adopt them.

Printed cheat sheets and PDFs. Every SAP project I've joined had one floating around. They're useful on day one and stale by month three. The moment SAP renames a transaction, splits one in two, or marks something as "do not use in S/4HANA," the cheat sheet starts misleading people.

The SAP Easy Access menu and Favorites. Complete in theory, unbrowsable in practice for anyone who doesn't already know the menu path. I've watched experienced users scroll for two minutes looking for something they could have typed in four characters, if only they remembered which four.

SE93 (Maintain Transaction Codes). Useful when you know part of the code or want to inspect the underlying program. Less useful when all you know is what the business process is called in plain English.

Internal wikis and Confluence pages. These work right up until the person who curated them leaves, or until S/4HANA introduces Fiori-app equivalents that nobody backfilled into the wiki.

Generic web search. Returns an ocean of forum posts of varying ages, half of them referencing transactions that have been deprecated or replaced. You usually find the answer. It just takes a lot of clicking for a four-character payoff.

What all of these methods share is an unspoken assumption: that the user already knows enough SAP vocabulary to translate intent into transaction code. For cross-functional users and new joiners, that assumption is exactly where the wheels come off.

What's different now

Two things changed recently that make the problem more tractable.

The first is that the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library has gotten noticeably better. SAP recently relaunched it at fal.cloud.sap (the older URL fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com still works for now). You can search by classic T-code and see the corresponding Fiori app or apps, the business role, the line of business, and the product version. For S/4HANA migration work, this has saved me a lot of guessing. It's the authoritative SAP source for the ECC-to-Fiori mapping question.

The second is that AI-assisted search has reached the point where natural-language queries actually work. Vector embeddings let a phrase like "create a purchase order" land in the same semantic neighborhood as ME21N, even though the two share no keywords. LLM re-ranking then surfaces the most likely match at the top of the list with a short explanation. Together, those two pieces produce a search experience that works for people who don't already speak SAP.

I've tried a few community-built tools in this space. One I keep coming back to is TCodeAI (tcodeai.vercel.app), a free web app that lets you search either by code (MIRO) or by intent ("post a vendor invoice") and ranks the results with a short note on why each match was suggested. It also includes Fiori-app mappings alongside the classic codes, which helps during the dual-stack ECC/S/4HANA period most teams are living through. I'm not affiliated with it. I find it a useful complement to the official Fiori Apps Library when I want a faster, conversational lookup.

Other tools are moving in the same direction, including SAP's own Joule copilot work and various module-specific search add-ons. I expect the space to keep maturing.

A few caveats are worth keeping in mind for any AI-assisted T-code tool:

  • Always verify against your own system before sharing a code with end users. What's available depends on your release, your activated business functions, and your authorizations.
  • Treat AI explanations as starting points, not gospel. These tools are good at narrowing the field, less good at understanding your specific configuration.
  • Deprecated and S/4HANA-restricted transactions are a moving target. Cross-check anything critical against the SAP S/4HANA Simplification List or the Simplification Item Catalog in the SAP ONE Support Portal.

A practical workflow for 2026

If I had to describe what a healthy T-code lookup workflow looks like today, it would be roughly this:

  1. For Fiori and S/4HANA mapping questions, start with the SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library.
  2. For "I know what I want to do but not what it's called," use whatever AI-assisted search tool fits your team's comfort level. TCodeAI is one option, but it isn't the only one.
  3. For deep validation, including release behavior, deprecation status, and replacement guidance, use the SAP S/4HANA Simplification Item Catalog and the linked SAP Notes via the SAP ONE Support Portal.
  4. Keep your team's internal cheat sheet small. Focus it on the codes specific to your customizations and Z-transactions.

The broader point is this. We've spent a long time treating T-code memorization as a kind of rite of passage in SAP. It doesn't have to be. The tooling has caught up enough that new joiners can focus on understanding the process and look up the codes the way the rest of us look up everything else.

I'd be curious to hear what others are using. If you've landed on a workflow that's cut down on "what's the T-code for...?" interruptions in your team, leave a note in the comments.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2026: Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions
entry.content:
Hello everyone, I am Noor Zehra Naqvi (many people call me Noor),  a Senior Director of Product Management at SAP Signavio. My focus area is where AI, data, and analytics meet.

Today I would like to introduce my SAP Sapphire session:

Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions | BTM1573

In the session I’ll share our strategy about AI Agent Excellence: how to discover, govern, maintain, and continuously improve agents so they deliver real value in business processes.

AI agents are becoming a new reality. Companies are increasing their investments, and we’re seeing more and more agents show up as part of the workforce, not only as a concept, but in real operational workflows.

From there, the key question becomes: where should you apply agents, and how do you scale them responsibly? This is where SAP Signavio helps by giving agents the right process and business context, and by supporting the guardrails organizations need so agents remain compliant, predictable, and aligned with how the business actually runs.

We’ll also dive into governance and transparency, how you can understand the impact of agents on your end-to-end processes, and how to validate conformance. For example, through what we call agent mining, you can see where agents touch a process, what variants they introduce, and whether those variants are conforming or not. This level of visibility is essential when you move from a few pilots to broad adoption.

To keep it practical, one of our customers, Albatha Group will join us to share how SAP Signavio supported their AI and AI Agents journey and helped create value in their organization. We’ll close with a sneak peek at our latest releases on Agentic AI.

Interested in learning more?

 

My biggest recommendation is: get started and experiment. Try out AI agents, see what’s possible, and quite literally get your hands dirty. At the same time, scaling agents inside an organization requires a framework: strong security, clear control mechanisms, and governance that prevents unexpected or unwanted behavior.

That’s why it’s worth investing time in understanding how SAP Signavio supports this journey, so you’re set up not just to build agents, but to operate them with confidence.

Also, beyond my own session), I’d encourage you to explore a few other Signavio-related sessions at SAP Sapphire, especially those that help you connect strategy with execution:

  • Unlocking the new frontier of agentic transformation with SAP Signavio | BTM2176
  • Deliver measurable transformation value confidently with SAP Signavio | BTM1574
  • Build a transformation capability that delivers measurable value | BTM1286
  • Crafting the future: The SAP Signavio solutions 2026 road map | BTM1715

 

Now let’s hear from you!

 

In the comments’ section, it would be great to hear where you are on your agentic AI journey:

  • How far along are you, are you still piloting, or already scaling?
  • Roughly how many agents do you have running today?
  • What are the biggest struggles, blockers and risks you’re seeing as you scale?

 

If you’re attending SAP Sapphire, I hope you’ll join the conversation, both during the event and in the comments. The shift to agentic AI is exciting, but the organizations that win will be the ones that pair innovation with strong process context, governance, and transparency.

entry.title: How to Set Up a Python Virtual Environment
entry.content:
Introduction:

A Python virtual environment is a local directory that maintains project dependencies distinct from the rest of the system. It prevents conflicts of version and provides a uniform development in various presuppositions. This is needed to create clean Python applications that are maintainable and scalable.

 

Open Visual studio and create a folder { To learn on How to step up Visual Studio for python programming , please refer my blog https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-blogs-posts/ai-steps-to-setup-python-programming/ba-p/14381631 }

 

 

Create Virtual environment using python -m venv {environment name}

 

 

 

Create folder for database , ui, and text file for requirements/dependencies

 

 

Add dependencies in requirement file

 

 

Install dependencies using command  

pip install -r {requirement text file name}

 

 

 

Activate Virtual environment

 

 

 

Summary 

A python virtual environment helps to isolate project dependencies and prevents conflicts with the rest of the system. It provides uniform, hygienic and controllable growth in various setups.

 

##If you found this blog helpful, please consider giving it a lik

entry.title: SAP Joule- The "Totally not an official ITSM connector"
entry.content:
SAP Joule isn’t a magical ITSM plug that sits between systems wearing a badge. It’s more like an enthusiastic translator at a networking event who says, “Oh, you’ve got an incident in ServiceNow? I’ll just whisper that into the API for you.”

So instead of “SAP Joule is an ITSM connector” Think: “SAP Joule intelligent assistant is that clever middle layer in SAP applications like S4HANA, Build Work Zone/Fiori that chats with ServiceNow via APIs, turns your casual complaints into structured incidents, and then politely updates them when new test results arrive—like a very overqualified messenger who never complains about copy-pasting JSON and knows how to get the job done through APIs while you just stand there wondering how it became so competent..”

In short: it’s not officially an ITSM connector , but it fakes it quite efficiently, powered entirely by APIs and good intentions. ( Using SAP Joule Studio in Build and custom Joule skills)

Incident creation from SAP Joule

When users interact with SAP Build Work Zone/Fiori or any SAP Joule enabled application, SAP Joule can act as an embedded AI assistant that interprets user requests, system alerts, or test outcomes. Instead of switching tools, users can simply describe an issue in natural language (for example, “login failure in payroll app after deployment or system dump”), and SAP Joule can:

 

  • Format the problem into a structured ITSM ticket
  • Create a new incident directly in ServiceNow

 

This reduces friction between business users and IT operations teams, ensuring incidents are logged consistently and quickly and with improved quality.

As shown below, SAP Joule asks to provide some mandatory inputs ( can be customized), which can be ingested in one single well drafted prompt, separated by commas and an incident is submitted in service now in Realtime.

Incident successfully created with the right inputs. Some inputs can be made mandatory to control the incident quality and later operational analysis.

Updating incidents with testing results

Beyond creation, SAP Joule can also manage the lifecycle of incidents. As users validate fixes or reproduce issues, they can feed results back into SAP Joule, which then:

  • Updates existing ServiceNow incidents with comments
  • Reflects status changes such as “In Testing,” “Resolved,” or “Reopened” (possible)

For example, after testing, a user can simply instruct SAP Joule to append results to an open incident. The update is immediately reflected in ServiceNow, ensuring real-time synchronization between SAP and ITSM systems.

Advantages of using SAP Joule for incident generation

Integrating SAP Joule with ServiceNow could provide several operational benefits:

  • Faster incident creation: Users can log incidents directly from business workflows without switching platforms
  • Reduced manual effort: Natural language processing eliminates the need for structured form filling
  • Improved accuracy: Context-aware AI reduces incomplete or inconsistent incident descriptions.
  • Seamless integration: Direct connection between SAP applications and ServiceNow improves end-to-end visibility
  • Real-time updates: Test results and comments can be pushed instantly into active incident

Summary

I only discussed two possibilities but the capabilities could go far beyond this.

  • Search incidents or Search known solutions
  • Read knowledge articles in service now (Document grounding with Service Now)
  • Auto assign Category/sub-category based on inputs or past incidents.

By positioning SAP Joule as an intelligent ITSM companion, organizations could bridge SAP-centric business processes with ServiceNow-based incident management. SAP Joule Embedded within SAP applications, could transform how incidents are created, enriched, and resolved—making IT operations more responsive, automated, and closely aligned with business activity.

Note:  GenAI was used for language refinement and tone improvement.

entry.title: ABAP AI: Creating Custom Generative AI Scenarios in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence in ABAP – Ready for Today

Artificial Intelligence capabilities are no longer limited to external services or side-by-side extensions. With recent SAP S/4HANA releases, AI scenarios can be consumed and modeled directly within ABAP, allowing existing applications and processes to be enhanced with Generative AI in an enterprise-ready way.

At the center of this enablement is the ABAP AI SDK powered by Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM). It provides the runtime APIs and lifecycle concepts required to define, manage, and consume AI scenarios from ABAP code in a consistent manner. This makes it possible for customers and partners to build Custom AI scenarios inside their own SAP S/4HANA systems, closely aligned with business logic and system architecture.

Why ABAP AI SDK and ISLM Matter

The ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM, enables ABAP developers to interact with Generative AI models without embedding provider-specific logic into applications. ISLM acts as the central framework and lifecycle component for AI scenarios, while the ABAP AI SDK exposes standardized APIs to consume these scenarios at runtime.

Beyond pure large language model (LLM) interactions, SAP AI Core provides an orchestration service. Orchestration in SAP AI Core is a managed service that enables unified access, control, and execution of generative AI models through standardized APIs, prompt templating, and configurable AI workflow components.

Using orchestration, LLM interactions can be enriched with additional capabilities such as data masking, content filtering, document grounding, and translation. The ABAP AI SDK powered by ISLM in ABAP can leverage both direct LLM interactions and orchestration features, allowing ABAP applications to consume advanced AI workflows while keeping governance and lifecycle management aligned with SAP standards. 

These capabilities are available as of SAP S/4 HANA 2023.

Learn by Doing: An SAP Developer Mission

To provide a structured, hands-on entry point, we offer an SAP Developer Mission that guides you through the setup and usage of Custom AI scenarios in ABAP.

The mission consists of a tutorial group, guiding you how to set up your SAP S/4 HANA system for custom AI scenarios and dedicated tutorials demonstrating how Custom GenAI scenarios are built and consumed from ABAP using the ABAP AI SDK, including orchestration-based interactions with generative models.

Who Is This Relevant For?

The mission is intended for:

  • ABAP developers who want to understand how Generative AI can be consumed from ABAP
  • Architects evaluating AI integration options for SAP S/4HANA
  • Development teams exploring Custom AI scenarios within their existing SAP landscapes

No prior AI expertise is required, but familiarity with ABAP development is assumed.

Getting Started

Ready? Let's explore the SAP Developer Mission 

👉Get Started with GenAI Development in SAP S/4HANA 

which provides a guided and practical introduction on  Generative AI scenario creation and consumption in ABAP.

Want to Learn More?

  • SAP Help Portal: ABAP AI & ISLM Documentation
  • SAP Community Page: Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management for SAP S/4HANA 
  • SAP Community Blog: Scaling Generative AI in SAP S/4HANA Enterprise-Ready Innovations with ISLM 
  • SAP Community Blog: Developing AI‑enabled applications with the ABAP AI SDK (Public Cloud & Steampunk)

entry.title: PORTAL - Understanding the Need for Secure & Responsible AI at SAP
entry.content:
This blog was co-authored with @Vimal_Dubey , whose contributions and insights were instrumental in shaping the perspectives presented in this article.

In one of the most crucial sectors – Aviation, safety relies on identifying and mitigating risks through robust, multi-layered systems designed to counter cognitive biases and organizational failures. Modern safety management integrates human factors, data-driven analytics, and rigorous testing to improve reliability and trust. Aircrafts need to go through thorough inspection and testing to prove their airworthiness before they are permitted to operate. Similarly, SAP’s Global AI Ethics Policy emphasizes principles such as fairness, privacy, robustness, transparency, and security. When SAP develops its own AI capabilities, these principles guide every phase—from dataset curation to deployment, with model benchmarking and evaluation being a critical part to ensure AI safety and security (see the keep_the_blue_side_up blogpost series).

One area of growing importance is SAP’s work with in-house foundation models. While many assume SAP only integrates external LLMs, the reality is different: SAP also builds its own foundational models tailored to business data, enterprise processes, and multi-modal use cases. One of these is the research work PORTAL.

For foundation models, it is important to ensure they are resilient and secure. Our recent evaluation of the research work PORTAL focuses exactly on this: understanding how PORTAL behaves when subjected to adversarial stress tests, privacy attacks, distribution shifts, and robustness checks. 
These findings are summarized here in a transparent and accessible way, based on the detailed technical analysis conducted internally.

Why Security and Robustness Testing Is Critical for Foundation Models

Foundation models learn from vast quantities of structured (tabular) and sometimes unstructured data. PORTAL is trained on substantial amount of tabular data. This gives foundation models impressive generalization capabilities but also exposes them to potential vulnerabilities.

To ensure that PORTAL meets SAP’s enterprise requirements, we evaluated it against a curated subset of the OWASP Top 10 for Machine Learning, focusing on attacks that are relevant to proprietary foundation models

 What Is PORTAL? 

PORTAL is an SAP research work for table-native foundation models. It:

  • learns from diverse, business-specific tabular datasets
  • supports multiple downstream tasks
  • can be fine-tuned for various enterprise scenarios
  • follows SAP’s policy-aligned AI safeguards by design

The following section presents the experiments conducted on PORTAL to assess the applicability of relevant OWASP threats within enterprise-focused environments.

Experiment : Sequential Fine-Tuning — Does PORTAL Retain Knowledge Safely?

We began by testing whether PORTAL can retain information across sequential training without leaking old data or overfitting to new data. We performed tests using the Breast Cancer dataset, which was available readily & had nearly equal distribution across various features & labels.

Case 1 — Extreme Distribution Shift (Label Flip)

Flipping class labels produced a dramatic drop in accuracy:

  • Before fine-tuning: 94.74%
  • After fine-tuning on flipped data: 8.07%

Interpretation: 
The model exhibited catastrophic forgetting, in that performance on the earlier fine-tuned task degraded substantially after adaptation to a strongly shifted distribution (label flip). This suggests that the model did not retain information from the previous fine-tuned dataset once trained on the new task, indicating overwriting of task-specific patterns during sequential fine-tuning. We emphasize that this observation is limited to the fine-tuning stage and does not provide evidence regarding memorization during pre-training.

Case 2 — Mild Feature Perturbations

We applied small additive and multiplicative Gaussian noise to simulate mild distribution shifts and fine-tuned the model on the perturbed data.

  • Before fine-tuning: 94.74%
  • After perturbation fine-tuning: 41.05%

Interpretation: 
PORTAL adapts better to controlled shifts. 
This demonstrates better generalization while still maintaining partial retention — the desirable behavior for enterprise adaptation, where gradual data drift is common. 

.      

Figure 1: Left: Case 1; Right: Case 2

Experiment : Membership Inference Attacks — Does PORTAL Leak Training Data? 
To evaluate privacy resilience, we analyzed whether PORTAL assigns systematically higher confidence scores to samples seen during recent fine-tuning compared to held-out samples. This experiment focuses exclusively on detecting potential membership signals in fine-tuning data and does not target memorization from pre-training.

Two datasets were used during fine-tuning:

  • 569-sample Breast Cancer dataset
  • 1000-sample augmented dataset (over-sampling)

For both settings, we compared the distributions of true-class confidence scores for member (train) and non-member (test/held out data) samples.

Findings: 

  • Smaller dataset → Larger confidence gap → Potentially higher privacy risk: In the smaller dataset setting, a mild shift in confidence distributions is observed, consistent with limited overfitting.
  • Larger dataset → Reduced confidence gap → Better privacy protection: In the augmented dataset setting, member and non-member confidence distributions exhibit strong overlap, indicating the absence of clear confidence-based separability.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Importantly, in neither setting does a reliable confidence threshold emerge that would enable effective membership inference. The increased overlap in the larger dataset further reduces residual leakage signals.

These results suggest that PORTAL exhibits low practical vulnerability to confidence-based membership inference attacks, and that increased data diversity and scale further enhance privacy robustness.  We emphasize that extracting memorized information from pre-training is substantially more challenging and remains outside the scope of this study.

Experiment : Knife-Edge Analysis — Understanding Feature Sensitivity

Knife-edge analysis helps identify features where very small changes can sharply flip the model’s prediction. This reveals whether the model is relying on balanced information or leaning too heavily on a single dominant signal. Two complementary tests were conducted:

  1. Global Sensitivity Test (Feature Shuffling / Permutation)
  2. Local Knife-Edge Test (Single Instance Feature Increment)

Comparative evaluation was performed on: PORTAL, Decision Tree, XGBoost

Case 1 -  Breast Cancer Dataset (Comparative Analysis) 

(The task is binary classification where, 0 = Malignant, 1 = Benign) 

Feature Importance Analysis: Before conducting knife-edge tests, we examined which features each model relied on most.

  • Portal: Mean Radius and Mean Perimeter, more weightage on Mean Radius
  • Decision Tree: Worst Radius has highest feature relevance
  • XGBoost: Worst Perimeter has the highest feature relevance 

The knife-edge experiments were then performed on the highest-ranked feature for each model.

.          . 

      PORTAL                                            Decision Tree                                                XGBoost

Global Sensitivity Test (Permutation): 

We shuffled the dominant feature for each model and measured accuracy change.

Run 1: 

       

PORTAL                                              Decision Tree                                                      XGBoost

Feature shuffled per model: PORTAL (Mean Radius), Decision Tree (Worst Radius), XGBoost (Worst Perimeter)

Run 

PORTAL Baseline (%) (Training) 

PORTAL Drop (%)(Testing) 

DT Baseline (%)(Training) 

DT Drop (%)(Testing) 

XGB Baseline (%)(Training) 

XGB Drop (%)(Testing) 

Run 1 

90.35 

13.16 

91.23 

7.02 

94.74 

−0.88 

Run 2 

87.72 

16.67 

91.23 

5.26 

94.74 

−0.88 

Run 3 

92.98 

17.54 

91.23 

3.51 

94.74 

−2.63 

Overall, the findings suggest that PORTAL relies more heavily on a single dominant feature, whereas tree-based models achieve greater robustness through distributed feature utilization.

Local Knife-Edge Test (Single Instance Analysis)

We selected a correctly predicted benign sample row:

  • mean radius = 11.13 (considered for PORTAL)
  • worst radius = 11.68 (considered for Decision Tree)
  • worst perimeter = 74.35 (considered for XGBoost)
  • true label = 1

We gradually increased the dominant feature (per model) from its original value in the selected sample up to the maximum observed value for that feature, while keeping all other features constant.

Observations:

     

PORTAL                                              Decision Tree                                             XGBoost

Comparative Sensitivity Summary:

Model 

Dominant Feature 

Global Accuracy Drop 

Local Flip Detected 

PORTAL  

Mean Radius 

High (−18.42%) 

Yes (at 16.03)

Minimal (+0.88%) 

No 

The results suggest that PORTAL places stronger emphasis on a single dominant scalar feature, whereas tree-based models (Decision Tree and XGBoost), despite assigning high importance to certain features, leverage a more distributed set of inputs during prediction.

Case 2 - Diabetes Dataset (PORTAL Corroboration Study)

The pattern repeated in the Diabetes dataset. 
When gradually changing Age, the prediction flipped from Diabetic → Non-diabetic at Age = 21, while adjusting Pregnancies had no impact. Again, one dominant feature drove the prediction.

    

Case 3 - Column Reordering

We also tested whether feature ordering affected predictions. Before reordering, occasional flips occurred during perturbations. After reordering, flips disappeared within the tested range — indicating that even column order influenced inference behaviour. 

    

For tree-based models (Decision Tree and XGBoost), column reordering was not applicable. These models bind feature indices during training and enforce strict feature-name alignment at inference time. As a result, predictions remain invariant to column order, and any mismatch in ordering typically raises a validation error rather than altering model output.

Overall Observation

Knife-edge evaluation across datasets shows that PORTAL tends to anchor its decisions around a few dominant scalar features. This can make the model efficient but also makes it more sensitive to noise and small perturbations. These insights help guide improvements in regularization and encourage more distributed feature learning, strengthening the model's robustness for real-world enterprise scenarios.

Experiment : Memorization Testing — Distinguishing True Learning from Learned Shortcuts

This experiment was conducted on the publicly available tutorial dataset containing multiple modality-based categories. We reduced the proportion of “audio” samples in the training set from 40% to 10% while increasing their share in the test set, forcing the model to classify predominantly unseen audio data. This means that, in the final case, the model was asked to classify 90% audio samples it had never seen before. Prior to splitting, explicit duplicate removal was performed between training and test sets to mitigate trivial data leakage.

Unexpected Outcome: Across every scenario, including when the model had seen only a small fraction of a category, the accuracy remained exceptionally high (99–100%). At first glance, this could be mistaken for excellent generalization. However, such results usually indicate the opposite.

Why This Matters

A model that performs perfectly on data it has never been trained on is often relying on:

  • memorized patterns from the initial training samples,
  • dataset artifacts, or
  • shortcut cues that do not represent true category understanding.

Why This Insight Is Important

If a model relies on memorized cues rather than meaningful learning, its performance could degrade significantly when exposed to real-world enterprise data, where such shortcuts may not exist. By identifying memorization tendencies early, we can:

  • Expand dataset diversity
  • Strengthen model robustness
  • Improve generalization across unseen business scenarios

Conclusion:

This evaluation helped us understand how PORTAL behaves under different security, robustness, and privacy conditions. Each experiment revealed meaningful insights - from how the model adapts to distribution shifts, to how it handles sensitive features, to where shortcut learning or memorization may appear. These findings directly support SAP’s goal of strengthening responsible, secure, and reliable foundation models for enterprise use. Analogically ensuring the flight is smooth, safe and reliable. PORTAL is another example of how AI transparency, robustness, and responsible evaluation help “keep the blue side up”.                                              

References:

  1. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.12715
  2. https://github.com/SAP-samples/data-attribute-recommendation-postman-tutorial/blob/main/Tutorial_Example_Dataset.csv
  3. https://help.sap.com/docs/data-attribute-recommendation
  4. https://github.com/SAP-samples/data-attribute-recommendation-postman-tutorial
  5. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2409.01380

entry.title: SAP Joule in Action: Redefining Business Intelligence
entry.content:
Introduction

The evolution of enterprise systems is entering a new phase one where AI is intergarted and actively  collaborates with users. With the introduction of SAP Joule, SAP has positioned itself at the forefront of this transformation by delivering a generative AI copilot across business applications.

SAP Joule AI represents a paradigm shift in how users interact with SAP systems such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors.

working on AI transformations, I have observed a clear shift: business users are no longer satisfied with transactional systems they expect intelligent assistants embedded directly into workflows. Joule aligns exactly with this expectation, bridging the gap between business intent and system execution.

What is SAP Joule?

SAP Joule is a generative AI copilot and more embedded across SAP’s cloud portfolio. It enables users to:

  • Interact with SAP systems using natural language
  • Automate complex workflows
  • Generate insights in real time
  • Enhance decision-making with contextual intelligence

 

Built on the SAP Business Technology Platform, Joule leverages AI models integrated with enterprise data while ensuring governance, security, and compliance.

From my implementation experience, one of the biggest challenge in SAP systems has been user navigation complexity. Joule’s conversational interface eliminates the need for deep transaction code knowledge, which is particularly valuable in large-scale deployments involving non-technical users.

Why SAP Joule Matters

Traditional ERP systems are transactional. SAP Joule makes them conversational and intelligent.

 

Key Value Drivers:

  • Productivity Boost: Reduce manual effort through AI-assisted automation
  • User Experience Transformation: Shift from UI-driven to intent-driven interactions
  • Faster Decision-Making: Real-time contextual insights
  • Process Optimization: Intelligent recommendations embedded in workflows

During SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, especially in success factor / supply chain environments, decision-making often depends on multiple reports and manual analysis. Joule has the potential to compress decision cycles from hours to minutes by generating real-time insights directly within the process

SAP Joule Architecture Overview

 

 

A typical Joule-enabled landscape includes:

Core ERP Layer

  • SAP S/4HANA (Digital Core)

AI & Extension Layer

  • SAP BTP (AI services, integration, extensions)

Data Layer

  • SAP Datasphere / SAP HANA Cloud

AI Copilot Layer

  • SAP Joule embedded in Fiori applications

Integration Layer

  • APIs, OData services, Event Mesh

 In S/4HANA  implementations , maintaining a clean core is critical. Joule fits well into this paradigm because it leverages BTP for AI processing, avoiding heavy customizations in the core ERP system.

Implementation Approach for SAP Joule

Phase 1: Readiness Assessment

  • Evaluate system landscape (ECC vs S/4HANA)
  • Identify high-value AI use cases
  • Assess data quality and governance maturity

 

Data readiness is the single biggest success factor. Without harmonized master data and clean transactional data, AI outputs , no matter how advanced , lose business relevance.

Phase 2: Platform Enablement

  • Activate SAP BTP services
  • Configure AI capabilities
  • Ensure connectivity with S/4HANA

Architectural side treat BTP not just as a platform, but as an AI innovation layer. Joule implementation makes it central to the architecture.

Phase 3: Use Case Identification

Common SAP Joule use cases:

Supply Chain

  • Demand forecasting insights
  • Exception handling automation

Procurement

  • Smart supplier recommendations
  • Contract summarization

HR

  • Job description generation
  • Leave insights

 

I see immediate value in navigation  where Joule can proactively suggest actions instead of users searching to find the panel.

Phase 4: Integration & Extension

  • Extend Joule capabilities using SAP BTP
  • Integrate with external AI models if required
  • Enable APIs for custom workflows

In complex enterprise landscapes (e.g., SAP + external logistics platforms), Joule can act as a unified intelligence layer, reducing dependency on multiple dashboards and tools.

Phase 5: Security & Governance

  • Role-based access control
  • Data privacy compliance
  • AI explainability and auditability

From an enterprise architecture standpoint, I see AI governance is not optional. Organizations must define:

  • What data Joule can access
  • How decisions are audited
  • How AI outputs are validated

This has become especially critical in regulated industries.

Phase 6: Change Management

  • User training for conversational AI
  • Adoption strategy
  • Continuous feedback loop

One underestimated challenge is user trust in AI. Adoption improves significantly when users understand:

  • How Joule generates responses
  • When to trust vs validate outputs

Best Practices for SAP Joule Implementation

  • Start with Business Value, Not Technology
  • Prioritize Clean Core in S/4HANA
  • Leverage Standard SAP AI Capabilities First
  • Ensure High Data Quality
  • Adopt Agile AI Deployment Approach

We Started with assistive AI (recommendations) before moving to autonomous AI (decision-making). This phased approach reduces risk and improves adoption.

Challenges to Consider

  • Data readiness and harmonization
  • User adoption resistance
  • AI governance and compliance
  • Integration complexity in hybrid landscapes

In real projects, the biggest blocker is not technology it’s organizational readiness. AI transformation requires alignment across IT, business, and leadership.

Future Outlook

SAP Joule will evolve into a central AI orchestrator, enabling:

  • Autonomous business processes
  • AI-driven decision intelligence
  • Multi-agent enterprise ecosystems

Given my work in Agentic AI, I see Joule as a foundation for multi-agent enterprise systems, where different AI agents collaborate across supply chain, finance, and operations.

Conclusion

SAP Joule represents a shift from transactional ERP systems to intelligent, AI-driven enterprises.

For SAP architects and leaders, the focus should be on aligning AI capabilities with business outcomes, ensuring governance, and building scalable AI-driven ecosystems.

From an SAP architect’s strategic perspective, Joule is not just another feature it is a strategic capability that will redefine how users interact with enterprise systems. Organizations that successfully adopt it will gain a significant competitive advantage in automation, decision-making, and operational efficiency.

entry.title: AI Agent Skills and their impact on SAP business processes
entry.content:
As usually happens to a consultant that works with SAP, business problems come to my desk. "This process doesn't work", "We can't work with this". 3 workshops later we have the usual solution, enhance a report, some extra columns while business swears X = Y only to be later confronted with X = Z. The usual. Wouldn't it be nice if business rules were managed by end users instead of us having to configure and code? That's what agent Skills (e.g Claude Skills) can do for you. 

This particular example deals with an export process, where we have the customer revenue but also a lot of service pro forma/invoices for port, terminal and custom costs which from a profitability perspective need to be posted to the same "process" as the revenue. Lots of supplier, different document layouts, all of them need to be matched to sales orders/deliveries. 

  • "The user" Solution - A business user looks at each pro forma, interprets it, goes into SAP and manually picks the sales orders/delivery items that the cost refers to, distributes the cost to each item and then creates a purchase order for it. Result: the user complains and says "I need 1 more FTE for this". 
  • "The custom" Solution - We develop a sophisticated system to interpret the pro forma, try to find what the items mean, where the reference to our process is, and then automate the distribution. Result: Each time we have a new invoice type or supplier we have to at least test, maybe develop a new exception and ... we need one more IT FTE (I'm joking, it's just extra work). 
  • "The AI" Solution - We give Claude to a user. He uploads the pro forma and asks Claude to convert to a purchase order format. He tells Claude what to do for that supplier, and what to do with the references, even how he wants to distribute the cost. And he finishes by saying "save this as an extract quote skill". Next time? Just ask upload and ask him to create a purchase order. Do you have another supplier? Tell them what to do with this one, and ask it to add the new behaviour particular to this vendor to the skill. 

Looks like magic right? But the most "magical" (or maybe concerning) thing about this is that there is no ABAP, no Fiori, no configuration. Just an AI Agent using MCP + OData to interact with a standard SAP system. To prove this not a magic scenario, look at this demo where I go through this very example: 

 

 

So I will just leave you with a call to action: Think about examples like this and how it will impact the way we do business processes and the skills that will become more relevant for functional consultants

Do I think there is still room for SAP consultants in the future? Of course, but I truly believe the role will become more and more a true consultant on business processes and less a producer of functional specifications.

And maybe, just maybe, we will finally have clean core.

entry.title: Your Journey to SAP Data Architect Certification Starts Now
entry.content:
The era of the "all-knowing" architect has arrived, and the blueprint for the enterprise of tomorrow is being written in real-time through the transformative power of Data and AI.

As a professional consultant or architect, you’ve spent your career designing complex systems, but the modern landscape now demands a visionary who can seamlessly bridge the gap between robust infrastructure and the autonomous world of Agentic AI. This is your moment to evolve from a traditional strategist into a high-impact leader capable of orchestrating a data-first world.

 

By enrolling in our newly released learning journeys: Becoming an SAP Data Architect and Mastering SAP Data Architecture. You are securing an undeniable career advantage in an AI-driven economy. These courses offer a structured, rigorous path designed specifically for experts ready to master modern paradigms like Data Mesh, Data Fabric, and Zero-ETL integration. You will gain the technical skills required to design scalable, high-performance data ecosystems that facilitate real-time access and advanced analytics across hybrid cloud environments.

 

Completing this journey validates your expertise on a global stage through industry-recognized certification: the SAP Certified Data Architect – SAP Business Data Cloud. You will master the integration of SAP’s open data ecosystem with external giants like Databricks and Google BigQuery, ensuring you can lead complex AI initiatives with absolute confidence. The future belongs to those who have the skills to build it - claim your seat at the forefront of innovation today.

 

Start your journey today!

entry.title: SAP AI: Beyond the Hype and Into the Real World
entry.content:
Three days in Excel hell. Twenty supplier bids. One AI agent that knows your data. This is the SAP AI story your vendor deck won't tell you — first in a series.

I've been on the digital transformation journey for more years than I care to admit. I truly believe that constant evolution is the only way to stay relevant in procurement, but lately, it feels like the word "transformation" has been completely hijacked by a single two-letter acronym: AI. Everywhere I turn, AI is the only topic in the room. It is being pitched as the ultimate cure-all, yet for most of us, the daily reality still involves wrestling with legacy systems and messy data.

After endless conversations about AI's impact today, where it is going, and how it fits into our daily lives, I've realized that what we need is not more noise, but a clear roadmap.

My intention with this series is to provide a solid understanding of where we are today, set some realistic expectations, and point out where the magic really happens when we get it right.

We will look at what SAP is doing broadly with Business AI, while zooming in specifically on the procurement space where the impact is most immediate. Each post in this series will focus on a specific piece of the puzzle or a real-world scenario, helping you not only navigate this AI transformation but ensuring you actually see tangible value at the end of it. And I promise: no marketing fluff and no buzzword bingo.

 

The Anatomy of Success: How the Pieces Actually Fit Together

Stop thinking about AI as a single feature you can switch on. It's a high-performance system built from three distinct, non-negotiable layers. Pull any one out and the whole thing fails to deliver value. Here's the blueprint:

 The three layers of the SAP AI stack. Remove any one and the system fails to deliver value.

Each layer has a clear job. BTP is where your code actually runs and your security lives. SAP Business Data Cloud is the part that prevents your AI from getting confused — it makes sure a "Supplier" in one system is the same thing as a "Vendor" in another. Joule is the conversational front door that takes your natural-language request and orchestrates the work across every back-end system. Everything else in this post depends on these three working together.

 

Four Flavors of SAP AI You'll Actually Encounter

Not all AI is created equal — and you'll meet at least four different flavors in any real SAP deployment. Each one solves a different problem and shows up in your workflow in a different way. Here's how to recognize them:

 Four flavors of SAP AI. Each one solves a different problem — and you'll meet all of them

 

Talk Is Cheap. Let's Look at a Real Scenario.

We've all heard the lofty promises of AI, but talk is notoriously cheap. Instead of another high-level theory, I want to show you a practical scenario that has already been built and is currently saving procurement experts hundreds of hours. This is not some futuristic concept waiting for a release date — it's how we're solving real-world complexity right now.

 The Bid Analysis Agent in action — what 80% of the heavy lifting looks like.

WHAT HAPPENS TODAY WITHOUT AI

You're stuck in Excel hell for three days. You're manually pivoting data, trying to run "what if" scenarios, and likely making a few coffee-fueled typos along the way. By the time you finally have an answer, the market data has already shifted and your analysis is effectively a historical document.

THE BASIC AI EXPERIENCE

You try to use a generic, public AI tool. You upload a spreadsheet and ask for an analysis. It gives you a shallow "cheapest is best" answer, hallucinates a few shipping routes that don't exist, and potentially leaks your sensitive pricing data to the public web. Fast, but dangerous — and completely lacking business context.

THE REALISTIC SAP AI FUTURE

You use a Bid Analysis Agent grounded in your actual Ariba data. You ask Joule to show the award scenario that minimizes risk if fuel prices spike. The agent evaluates the trade-offs, highlights bid outliers, and suggests a data-driven strategy in seconds by factoring in unit prices, shipping, and risk data. You're still the decision-maker — but the AI has handled 80% of the analytical heavy lifting for you.


Straight Talk: Paying the Data Tax

AI in 2026 is not about replacing the procurement professional. It is about capacity. It's about getting that 20% to 30% of your week back from repetitive, manual work.

But let's be real about the Data Tax. I'm not suggesting every organization is starting from scratch. In fact, most customers I talk to have done a great job with their supplier and master data. The real mess usually hides elsewhere: in the offline spreadsheets, the unstructured sourcing history, and the fragmented procurement processes that haven't quite made it into the system yet.

 

Two data realities. One of them turns your AI into a confident liar.

Good data is structured, centralized, and consistent. It means having one version of the truth, so that when you ask an AI for a risk assessment, it isn't hallucinating because it found three different versions of the same contract.

Bad data is the fragmented manual sprawl — the tribal knowledge and the manual workarounds that live outside your digital core.

When you try to use AI with bad data, the implications are simple: the AI becomes "confidently wrong." It will give you an answer, but that answer will be based on a fragment of reality. Paying the Data Tax is not a one-time activity or a weekend cleanup. It's an ongoing evolution in how you store and leverage your information — a commitment to continuous improvement where your AI outputs steadily improve as your data strategy matures.

You simply cannot ignore the data layer of the architecture and expect the interaction layer to give you accurate results.

What's Next

Moving forward, this series will dive deep into the specific AI scenarios and features that drive actual savings in both time and money. I'll be sharing not just what we're building at SAP, but also the real-world best practices I'm seeing from other leading organizations that are successfully closing the "execution gap." There's a wealth of peer knowledge out there that can benefit us all.

I have specific use cases planned, but I'm always interested in your perspective. If there's a particular challenge you're facing or a piece of the puzzle you want to see solved, let me know what you'd like me to cover in future chapters.

entry.title: AI Product Management: A Field That Doesn't Have Its Frameworks Yet
entry.content:
Series introduction - AI Product Management for SAP Practitioners

I have been building products at SAP, at Walmart, and in regulated healthcare AI for over fifteen years. I am also a trained physician. That combination gives me an unusual vantage point on what is happening right now in AI product management, and I want to share it in a series of posts written specifically for people building on SAP's AI stack.

This is the first post. It is not a how-to. It is a map of the territory, an orientation to the problems this series will dig into, and an honest accounting of why product management for agentic AI is genuinely different from what most of us were trained to do.

The Question Nobody Asks at the Start

Here is the question that should open every agentic AI project, and almost never does: how do you design a system that is probabilistic, acts on its own, and does not know how to admit when it does not know what to do?

That question sounds abstract. It is not. Every team shipping a Joule extension, a BTP-hosted agent, or an autonomous workflow in SAP Build is answering it, whether they realize it or not. The answer is baked into their architecture choices, their evaluation frameworks, their monitoring setup, and their change management approach. Most teams answer it implicitly, by not answering it at all, and discover the problem six months after go-live when the agent is doing something nobody designed it to do.

The probabilistic part is the piece that catches people first. Traditional software is deterministic: given the same inputs, you get the same outputs. You can test it, certify it, and trust the certification. An AI system gives you a distribution of outputs. Most of the time it lands in the right part of the distribution. Sometimes it does not. The conditions that push it into the wrong part of the distribution are not always predictable in advance. This changes what testing means, what monitoring means, and what production readiness means.

The autonomous action part is what makes agentic AI qualitatively different from a recommendation engine or a classification model. An agent does not just output a suggestion for a human to act on. It acts. It calls tools, modifies records, sends communications, triggers workflows. When it is wrong, it does not just produce a wrong answer. It produces a wrong action, and wrong actions have a way of compounding before anyone notices.

The part about not admitting uncertainty is the most insidious. Large language models are fluent. They produce coherent, confident-sounding output even when they are operating outside the distribution of their training data, even when the question has no good answer, even when the right response would be to stop and ask a human. Fluency without calibration is the failure mode nobody demonstrates in a demo and everybody discovers in production.

 

The Kindergarten Problem

When my children were in kindergarten, I noticed something that has stayed with me ever since. A classroom of five-year-olds, supervised, is a manageable system. Remove the teacher for ten minutes, and you come back to a different situation than you left.

This is not a metaphor about AI being childlike. It is a structural observation about supervised versus unsupervised systems. Children in a supervised environment have access to a feedback loop: they make a choice, someone responds, they adjust. Remove the supervision and the feedback loop closes on itself. The kids are still making choices, but the signal that tells them when a choice was wrong is gone. So the choices drift.

Agentic systems behave the same way. An agent running within a well-designed supervisory loop, one that provides clear feedback, catches errors before they compound, and escalates uncertainty to human judgment, performs very differently from the same agent running without it. The model is identical. The environment is different. The outcomes diverge significantly over time.

Most enterprise AI deployments focus intensely on the agent and very little on the supervisory system around it. The evaluation is of the model. The monitoring is of the latency. The production readiness checklist covers security and compliance. Nobody asks: what does it look like when this agent has been running for six months and the humans who are supposed to supervise it have stopped paying close attention because it has been reliable? What does recovery look like when it fails in a way that the monitoring did not catch?

These are not exotic edge cases. They are the predictable outcome of systems that were designed without the supervisory layer in mind.

You Are Not a Bridge Anymore

For most of my career, the core job of a product manager was translation. You understood customer language and engineering language and you moved between them, converting benefits into capabilities and requirements into roadmap. You were the bridge.

AI has not eliminated that job. It has made it automatable. The artifacts that used to consume 60 to 70 percent of a PM's week, the PRDs, the synthesis of customer feedback, the competitive analysis, the roadmap documentation, can now be drafted in a fraction of the time. That time is not going to disappear. The question is what you do with it.

The answer I have arrived at, and that this series will explore in depth, is that the PM's job has expanded from operating the bridge to architecting the system around the bridge. You are now responsible for two things you were not responsible for before: the design of the agent's autonomy boundary (what it can decide alone, what it must escalate, what it must never do), and the design of the supervisory system around it (how humans know what the agent is doing, how they intervene when it goes wrong, how the organization maintains the judgment to catch errors it never trained for).

This is not a skill most product managers have been asked to develop. It is the skill that separates teams that deploy agentic AI successfully from teams that quietly decommission it eighteen months later.

The Autonomy Ladder

One of the frameworks I developed in writing about this topic is what I call the Autonomy Ladder. It has three rungs, and most teams are on the wrong one for the wrong reasons.

The first rung is the suggestion engine. The AI recommends and a human acts. Every decision passes through a human before anything happens. This is the safest configuration and also the most common starting point. It is also the one most teams are impatient to leave, because the efficiency gains are modest when a human has to review every output before acting on it.

The second rung is the copilot. The agent acts and a human reviews. The system takes action autonomously, but every action is logged and surfaced for human audit within a defined window. Errors are caught in retrospect rather than prevented in advance. This configuration works when the cost of a wrong action is recoverable and the volume of actions makes pre-review impractical. Most enterprise agentic deployments today are targeting this rung, and many are discovering that the audit workflow is harder to design well than the agent itself.

The third rung is the autonomous actor. The agent acts within defined boundaries with no routine human review. This is where the efficiency gains are greatest and where the governance requirements are also greatest. An agent operating here needs a precisely scoped autonomy boundary, a real-time monitoring layer that catches anomalous behavior, a clear escalation path for situations outside the boundary, and a recovery workflow for when it acts wrongly within the boundary. Most teams that reach this rung reach it before they have designed those four things.

The mistake most teams make is not choosing the wrong rung. It is moving up the ladder on a schedule rather than on demonstrated evidence that the rung below is working. Utah recently became the first US state to approve an AI agent for autonomous prescription renewals. The threshold they set for moving to autonomous operation was 250 reviewed interactions. That is a count, not a safety criterion. It says nothing about whether the agent correctly identifies the patients for whom autonomous renewal is inappropriate. The ladder was climbed on schedule, not on evidence. A future post in this series will go deep on how to earn the move up, rather than schedule it.

What This Series Covers

The posts that follow will go deep on each of these problems. The frameworks are universal; they apply to any team building agentic AI regardless of the stack. Where I have examples from the SAP ecosystem, Joule, BTP, SAP Build, and SAP Business Data Cloud, I will use them. But the problems being solved are not SAP-specific. They are the problems every product manager faces when the software they are responsible for starts taking actions in the world.

I will cover the suitability question: not every problem deserves an agent, and the evaluation framework for deciding is different from anything most PMs have used before. I will cover the cost model: the economics of agentic AI do not look like traditional software and most business cases get them wrong in predictable ways. I will cover evaluation and observability: how you measure an agent in production in a way that actually tells you whether it is working. I will cover the supervisory system design: what it means to build the human side of an agentic deployment, not just the agent. And I will cover the security layer that most teams discover too late, when an agent that passed every review has been quietly doing something nobody authorized.

This is the work. It is harder than it looks in the demo. It is also more important than most organizations currently treat it.

The agent is a powerful tool. The job of the product manager is to design the system it operates inside, not just the tool itself.

The next posts is Seven Things You Need to Understand About AI Before You Build With It 

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: AI Product Management: Seven Things You Need to Understand About AI Before You Build With It
entry.content:
Post 1 — AI Product Management series

Before the architecture review. Before the sprint planning. Seven concepts every product manager needs to have loaded before any agentic AI project begins. Not because they are advanced. Because they are prerequisites. Every design mistake I have seen in enterprise agentic deployments traces back to a team that skipped one of these.

01. Suggestion vs. Action

Label every use case. Everything downstream depends on it.

There is a categorical difference between an AI system that surfaces a recommendation and one that takes an action. A system that drafts an email and waits for the sales rep to send it is a different product, with a different accountability model and a different failure mode, than a system that sends the email autonomously. Both can be built. Neither is inherently wrong. But they require different designs, different governance, and different contracts with the user.

The mistake is treating this as a slider you can adjust later. It is a foundational decision. When a user believes the system is making suggestions and the system is actually taking actions, the trust mismatch produces exactly the kind of failure that ends up in a post-incident review. Name the contract before engineering starts. Write it into the spec. Check it at every design review.

02. Deterministic vs. Probabilistic

Know where your governance burden lives in any hybrid system.

Traditional enterprise software is deterministic: the same input produces the same output every time. You can test it with a finite set of cases, cover the relevant conditions, and trust the certification. An AI system is probabilistic: it samples from a distribution of possible outputs. The same input produces a different output on a different run. This is not a bug. It is the fundamental behavior of a system built on learned patterns rather than explicit rules.

The practical implication: most enterprise systems are hybrids. The billing rules are deterministic. The customer service agent sitting on top of them is probabilistic. The inventory system is deterministic. The demand forecasting model feeding it is probabilistic. Knowing which parts of your system are probabilistic tells you where your governance burden lives. The deterministic parts can be tested with a finite set of cases. The probabilistic parts require ongoing monitoring, statistical testing, and a fundamentally different standard of evidence for what "working" means.

A story that says "the system correctly identifies high-risk transactions" cannot have a pass/fail acceptance criterion for a probabilistic component. It needs a performance threshold, a measurement methodology, and a monitoring plan.

03. The Four-Layer Stack

Foundation model, orchestration, tools, and context. Especially context.

When your engineering team says "we're adding an AI feature," they are usually talking about the first layer. There are four.

The foundation model is the large language model from a provider. Your team selects it but does not control it. The orchestration layer is the code your team writes to coordinate agent behavior: which tools to call, in what sequence, what to do when something fails. The tool layer is every system the agent can access, every API it can call, every action it can take. The context layer is what the agent reads before it acts: the prompt, the retrieved documents, the customer record, the previous steps in the workflow.

Most teams invest heavily in layers one through three. They design layer four by accident. This is the most expensive design error in agentic AI. A model without the right context is not a useful model; it is a confident model that will fill the gaps in its knowledge with plausible-sounding inferences. Intelligence without context is just confidence.

04. Context Is the Product

Name what must travel with the data. Reject architectures that assume the agent will figure it out.

When data moves from its source system into the agent's context, it leaves things behind. A customer record travels with the account value, the ticket history, and the product usage data. What does not travel: the contractual SLA negotiated individually, the active legal review that makes anything the agent says to this customer discoverable, the "high-value" flag computed by a scoring model retired six months ago.

The data the agent sees is accurate. The context that gives the data meaning is below the waterline, invisible to the agent.

The design response is an explicit context contract: a named specification of what context must travel with the data when it moves into the agent's working memory. The question to ask at every design review is this: what does this agent need to know that is not in the data it will receive? Who owns that information? Can it be surfaced? If not, what decisions should the agent be blocked from making without it?

Architectures that assume the agent will figure out what it needs should be rejected at the design review, not discovered as a problem after the first consequential error.

05. Specificity, Sensitivity, AUC

The threshold is a PM decision, not a model setting.

For any agentic feature that makes a classification, three statistical terms determine product behavior. Sensitivity is the proportion of real positive cases the model correctly identifies. High sensitivity catches most real cases but produces more false alarms. Specificity is the proportion of true negatives the model correctly dismisses. High specificity produces fewer false alarms but misses more real cases. AUC summarizes how well the model separates positives from negatives across all threshold settings.

The threshold is the point at which the model decides to act. Lower it and the model acts more often. Raise it and the model acts less often. Your data science team cannot set it for you. Only you know what a false positive costs your users versus what a missed case costs them. A fraud model set too aggressively blocks legitimate transactions. Set too loosely, it lets fraud through. The right threshold reflects your actual cost structure, not the threshold that maximizes a statistical metric on a benchmark dataset.

This is a product decision with business consequences. Own it.

06. The Autonomy Ladder

Autonomy is earned. Define demotion triggers before launch.

In my previous post I described the three rungs: Suggestion Engine, Copilot, Autonomous Actor. The ladder matters here because it is the mechanism by which the classification in concept 01 becomes a governance commitment.

The most expensive mistake teams make is moving up the ladder on a schedule rather than on demonstrated evidence that the rung below is working. Utah recently approved an AI agent for autonomous prescription renewals after 250 reviewed interactions. That is a count, not a safety criterion. The ladder was climbed on schedule, not on evidence.

The question that should appear in every go/no-go document: what would cause us to move this agent back down a rung? Define the demotion triggers before launch, not after the first incident. The conditions that justify autonomous operation are also the conditions that, when violated, require pulling back. If you cannot name them before deployment, you do not yet have enough information to deploy.

07. Two Channels, Not One

Design the agent and the supervisory system that governs it. Both. Before you ship.

Here is a question most agentic products cannot answer: how does a supervisor intervene when the agent is doing something wrong?

Most teams do not have a good answer because they only built one channel. Channel one is the path from agent to user: outputs, recommendations, status updates, approval requests. This is the visible product, the one that gets roadmap attention and launch criteria. Channel two is the path from human supervisor back to the agent: escalation, override, configuration change, governance intervention. Channel two carries the authority flow. It is how a human takes control when something is wrong.

Without channel two, there is no real supervision. There is only observation. You can watch what the agent is doing, but you cannot do anything about it in time. A well-designed agent with no channel two is a liability waiting to scale. If you cannot interrupt the agent, override a decision, or pause an in-flight workflow, the only available response to a problem is a full shutdown. That is not supervision. That is damage followed by an emergency shutdown.

Design both channels before you ship either.

The prerequisite, not the checklist

These seven concepts are not a compliance checklist. They are the mental model the PM carries into every design decision, every sprint review, every stakeholder conversation about an agentic feature. The team that has these loaded makes different decisions at every stage, earlier, before the architectural defaults have hardened into constraints.

The next post in this series goes deeper on the first consequential decision: is this problem actually suited for an agent? Not every problem that can be solved with an agent should be. The evaluation framework is different from anything most PMs have used before.

The next posts is Not Every Problem Deserves an Agent

This post is part of the AI Product Management series on SAP Community. The series is based on "Agentic AI for Product Managers," a lifecycle framework for building, evaluating, and governing agentic systems.

entry.title: Building Agentic Hybrid RAG with Knowledge Graphs + Vectors - Developer Guide
entry.content:
Who this is for: Python developers on SAP BTP who want to build a smart document Q&A system. No prior knowledge of knowledge graphs or SPARQL needed.

What you will build: A FastAPI service that reads PDFs, stores the data in two ways (a knowledge graph and a vector store) inside SAP HANA Cloud, and answers questions using both stores at the same time — deployed to Cloud Foundry.

Stack: Python 3.11 · FastAPI · LangChain · SAP HANA Cloud · SAP AI Core (Claude via Bedrock) · Cloud Foundry

Figure 1 — Agentic Hybrid RAG Architecture

Step 1 — Understanding the Problem

Before building, let’s look at why existing approaches fall short when you need accurate answers from documents.

1.1 Why Pure Vector RAG Breaks on Exact Facts

What vector RAG actually does

Vector RAG works like this: it cuts your documents into pieces (chunks), converts each piece into a list of numbers (a vector), and when you ask a question, it finds the pieces whose numbers are closest to your question’s numbers.

The problem: it finds text that talks about the topic — not necessarily the exact fact you asked for.

The chunk boundary problem — a real example

Imagine your MSDS document contains this section:

Section 7 — Handling and Storage

The product must be stored below 25°C in a well-ventilated area.
Keep away from sources of ignition. Flash point: 42°C.
Containers must be grounded to prevent static discharge.

When you split this document into 500-token pieces, this text ends up mixed with the previous section about composition percentages. The flash point 42°C is in there somewhere, but buried in noise.

Now ask: “What is the flash point of this product?”

The top-5 retrieved chunks might look like this:

[score=0.91] "...The product must be stored below 25°C in a well-ventilated
             area. Keep away from sources of ignition. Flash point: 42°C.
             Containers must be grounded..."
[score=0.84] "...Flammability classification: Flam. Liq. 3. See Section 9
             for physical and chemical properties. Refer to SDS for complete
             hazard data..."
[score=0.79] "...Physical state: liquid. Colour: clear. Odour: mild petroleum.
             Boiling point: 155°C. Melting point: <-20°C. Vapour pressure..."
[score=0.74] "...Section 9 — Physical and Chemical Properties. All values
             measured at 20°C unless otherwise stated..."
[score=0.71] "...For fire-fighting: CO2, dry powder, foam. Do not use water
             jets directly on burning liquid. Flash point determined by..."
